title: Decimals
shortDescription: A parent's guide to teaching decimals through money and measurement, lining up the decimal point, and fixing the mistake almost every child makes.
importance: Decimals show up everywhere in a child's world, especially money with dollars and cents, and measurements like meters and centimeters. They are really just fractions wearing a different outfit, since one half is the same as 0.5. A child who sees that a decimal and a fraction describe the same value makes a big leap forward. Money is the perfect entry point because every child already understands it, and from there the whole topic feels concrete instead of abstract.
howToTeach: Start with money, because dollars and cents are decimals your child already meets every day at the store.
Explain that the decimal point separates the whole part from the part that is smaller than one whole.
Practice reading numbers correctly, so 3.45 is read as three and forty-five hundredths, not three point forty-five.
In addition and subtraction, make the golden rule lining up the decimal points one under the other before doing anything else.
Show that you can add zeros at the end without changing the value, since 0.5 is exactly the same as 0.50.

examples.explanation: Line up the points. The part after the point: 5 + 3 = 8. The whole part: 2 + 1 = 3. So the answer is 3.8.
Think of it as money: 5 dollars minus 2 dollars and 75 cents leaves 2 dollars and 25 cents, which is 2.25.
Fill in a zero to compare, so 0.5 becomes 0.50. Now it is clear that 0.50 is bigger than 0.45, just like 50 cents beats 45 cents.
Three tenths plus seven tenths makes ten tenths, and ten tenths is exactly one whole. It is a tidy result that shows how the parts complete a whole.

commonMistakes: Not lining up the decimal points in addition and subtraction, so digits get added in the wrong column.
Thinking that 0.5 and 0.50 are different numbers, even though they are exactly the same value.
Forgetting to fill in zeros when comparing, which makes it hard to decide which number is larger.
Reading the number incorrectly, which is a clue that the idea of tenths and hundredths is not yet solid.
parentTips: Practice with real money at the store, since handling coins makes decimals concrete and memorable.
Show a ruler with centimeters and millimeters as another everyday example of parts of a whole.
Keep an eye on the decimal point, because most decimal mistakes start with lining it up wrong.
When your child is stuck, translate the problem into dollars and cents and suddenly it makes sense.
Have your child read numbers out loud, because saying a number correctly usually means they understand it.
